(**US 20**) is a part of the [United States Numbered Highway System](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highway_System "United States Numbered Highway System") that runs from [Newport, Oregon](/wiki/Newport,_Oregon "Newport, Oregon"), to [Boston, Massachusetts](/wiki/Boston,_Massachusetts "Boston, Massachusetts"). In the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), US 20 extends 372.32 miles (599.19 km) from the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") at [Ripley](/wiki/Ripley,_New_York "Ripley, New York") to the [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") state line in the [Berkshires](/wiki/Berkshires "Berkshires"). US 20 is the longest surface road in New York. It runs near the [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") shore from Ripley to Buffalo and passes through the southern suburbs of Buffalo, the [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes"), the glacial moraines of [Central New York](/wiki/Central_New_York "Central New York"), and the city of [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") before crossing into Massachusetts. US 20 connects to all three major north–south Interstate Highways in [Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"): [Interstate 390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") (I-390) near [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York"), [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_New_York "Interstate 81 in New York") south of [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), and [I-87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)") in Albany by way of [Fuller Road Alternate](/wiki/NY_910F "NY 910F").

**U.S. Route 20** (**US 20**) is a part of the [United States Numbered Highway System](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highway_System "United States Numbered Highway System") that runs from [Newport, Oregon](/wiki/Newport,_Oregon "Newport, Oregon"), to [Boston, Massachusetts](/wiki/Boston,_Massachusetts "Boston, Massachusetts"). In the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), US 20 extends 372.32 miles (599.19 km) from the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") at [Ripley](/wiki/Ripley,_New_York "Ripley, New York") to the [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") state line in the [Berkshires](/wiki/Berkshires "Berkshires"). US 20 is the longest surface road in New York. It runs near the [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") shore from Ripley to Buffalo and passes through the southern suburbs of Buffalo, the [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes"), the glacial moraines of [Central New York](/wiki/Central_New_York "Central New York"), and the city of [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") before crossing into Massachusetts. US 20 connects to all three major north–south Interstate Highways in [Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"): [Interstate 390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") (I-390) near [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York"), [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_New_York "Interstate 81 in New York") south of [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), and [I-87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)") in Albany by way of [Fuller Road Alternate](/wiki/NY_910F "NY 910F").

With the exception of Albany, it passes directly through no major cities of the state, bypassing Syracuse and [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York") by great distances to the south while the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") and [New York State Route 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") (NY 5), which share its corridor, pass right through or close to them. It skirts the southern and eastern suburbs of [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"). It is, however, a major artery in many of the outlying areas it passes through in the hilly fringes of the [Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Allegheny_Plateau "Allegheny Plateau"), often expanding to four lanes (it has no [limited-access](/wiki/Limited-access "Limited-access") sections, although many intersecting roads are [grade-separated](/wiki/Grade-separated "Grade-separated")) with extensive commercial strip development. One of these sections, the easterly of two [concurrencies](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with NY 5 across the northern Finger Lakes, is the second-longest surface-road concurrency in New York, behind only the concurrency of [I-86](/wiki/Interstate_86_in_New_York "Interstate 86 in New York") and [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17") in the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier"),[[1]](#cite_note-2014tdr-1) extending 67 miles (108 km) from Avon to [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn,_New_York "Auburn, New York").

From [Oneida County](/wiki/Oneida_County,_New_York "Oneida County, New York") to Albany, the road follows the historic **Cherry Valley Turnpike**, built at the beginning of the 19th century to connect Albany and, at the time, the important villages of [Sharon Springs](/wiki/Sharon_Springs,_New_York "Sharon Springs, New York"), [Cherry Valley](/wiki/Cherry_Valley_(village),_New_York "Cherry Valley (village), New York"), [Richfield Springs](/wiki/Richfield_Springs,_New_York "Richfield Springs, New York"), [Cazenovia](/wiki/Cazenovia_(village),_New_York "Cazenovia (village), New York"), and [Skaneateles](/wiki/Skaneateles_(village),_New_York "Skaneateles (village), New York"). US 20 itself was assigned in 1926 and was the state's main east–west route from that time until the Thruway was completed in the 1950s.

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route description")]

US 20 enters [Western New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York") closely paralleling the [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") shoreline, the Thruway and NY 5. Passing through the southeastern suburbs of Buffalo, it assumes a due-east heading at [Depew](/wiki/Depew,_New_York "Depew, New York"), taking it to the NY 5 overlap in [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York"). The two roads pass through many of the communities at the north ends of the larger [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes"), splitting in [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn,_New_York "Auburn, New York"). Through [Central New York](/wiki/Central_New_York "Central New York") and the [Central New York Region](/wiki/Central_New_York_Region "Central New York Region") to its east, US 20 drifts south into the rugged upper reaches of the [Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Allegheny_Plateau "Allegheny Plateau"), distancing itself from the Thruway and NY 5 by as much as 20 miles (32 km) at some points.[[3]](#cite_note-2009map-3)

In the [Capital District](/wiki/Capital_District_(New_York) "Capital District (New York)"), the three routes all converge again, and US 20 goes right through downtown [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), the largest city along its route in New York. Just before crossing the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), US 20 is joined again by [US 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9_in_New_York "U.S. Route 9 in New York") for its second-longest concurrency, which ends just before the Thruway's Berkshire section in Schodack Center. From there, it drifts southward into the [Berkshires](/wiki/Berkshires "Berkshires"), crossing the [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") state line west of [Pittsfield](/wiki/Pittsfield,_Massachusetts "Pittsfield, Massachusetts").[[3]](#cite_note-2009map-3)

All but 5.60 miles (9.01 km) of US 20's 372-mile (599 km) alignment in New York is maintained by the [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") (NYSDOT). In [Cayuga County](/wiki/Cayuga_County,_New_York "Cayuga County, New York"), the 1.21-mile (1.95 km) section of US 20 in Auburn between [NY 38A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_38A "New York State Route 38A") and the eastern city line is maintained by the city of Auburn.[[4]](#cite_note-inv-cayuga-4) To the east in [Albany County](/wiki/Albany_County,_New_York "Albany County, New York"), the 4.39 miles (7.07 km) of US 20 in Albany from the western city line to the north end of the [NY 32](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_32 "New York State Route 32") overlap is also city-maintained.[[5]](#cite_note-inv-albany-5)

### Western New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Western New York")]

From the [state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") to the [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") suburbs, US 20 is largely a two-lane through road on the same northeastern heading it has followed most of the way from [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"). It serves as the main street of the few communities it passes directly through. In the Buffalo area, US 20 begins to head more east, widening to four lanes and becoming a busy regional artery that intersects many other roads of major and minor importance. For the five miles (8.0 km) leading into [Depew](/wiki/Depew,_New_York "Depew, New York"), it runs due north along with [NY 78](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_78 "New York State Route 78") as part of Transit Road, a busy commercial strip east of the city. At Depew, US 20 leaves Transit Road and begins its journey east across the state. The surrounding countryside returns to farmland by the [Genesee County](/wiki/Genesee_County,_New_York "Genesee County, New York") line.[[3]](#cite_note-2009map-3)

#### Lake Erie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lake Erie")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/END%21_NY_239.jpg/250px-END%21_NY_239.jpg)](/wiki/File:END!_NY_239.jpg)

The former northern terminus of [NY 239](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_239 "New York State Route 239") at US 20 in Alden

US 20 enters New York immediately after passing through [State Line, Pennsylvania](/wiki/State_Line,_Erie_County,_Pennsylvania "State Line, Erie County, Pennsylvania"). It remains on the northeasterly course it has been following as [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_New_York "Interstate 90 in New York"), now the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway"), veers between it and what is now [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") in order to remain on level ground. This section of US 20 hugs the foot of the [beach ridge](/wiki/Beach_ridge "Beach ridge") to the south of [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie"), which is sometimes visible from sections of the highway, in [Chautauqua County](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York"). A mile and a half (2.4 km) east of the state line, Shortman Road[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] (unsigned [NY 950D](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_950D "New York State Route 950D")[[6]](#cite_note-2009log-6)) leaves to the left for exit 61, the westernmost interchange on the Thruway. Shortly afterward, US 20 reaches its first settlement in New York, the [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)") of [Ripley](/wiki/Ripley,_New_York "Ripley, New York"), where it intersects [NY 76](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_76 "New York State Route 76"), the first touring route along US 20 in the state.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Another eight miles (13 km) of two-lane rural road, crossed at its midpoint by the onetime [New York Central Railroad](/wiki/New_York_Central_Railroad "New York Central Railroad") and now [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation") mainline, brings US 20 into its first incorporated community, the [village](/wiki/Village_(New_York) "Village (New York)") of [Westfield](/wiki/Westfield_(village),_New_York "Westfield (village), New York"). Here, [NY 394](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_394 "New York State Route 394") intersects US 20 in the middle of town on its way to the Thruway's next exit and the hamlet of [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona,_New_York "Barcelona, New York"), where it encounters NY 5 and the lake. Beyond Westfield, US 20 begins to drift south, coming further inland as it heads northeast. 16 miles (26 km) to the east, it reaches [Fredonia](/wiki/Fredonia,_New_York "Fredonia, New York"), the largest village along its route so far. 20 intersects with [NY 60](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_60 "New York State Route 60") at Reed Corners at the eastern edge of Fredonia, which heads north to the neighboring city of [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk,_New_York "Dunkirk, New York"). Two miles (3.2 km) east, [NY 39](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_39 "New York State Route 39") becomes the first state route to end at US 20.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It makes a loop of nearly 100 miles (160 km) through the interior of the state to return to US 20 in the [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes").[[3]](#cite_note-2009map-3)

Past this junction, the Thruway and US 20 begin to converge in flatter country until they cross as US 20 veers northward one mile (1.6 km) south of [Silver Creek](/wiki/Silver_Creek,_New_York "Silver Creek, New York"). [US 20 Truck](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_Truck_(Silver_Creek,_New_York) "U.S. Route 20 Truck (Silver Creek, New York)") splits off from US 20 in the center of town and rejoins its parent northeast of the village. This portion of the truck route is cosigned as [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5"), and the first of two [overlaps](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") between US 20 and NY 5 begins here. The road resumes its northeasterly course to an access road to the Thruway's exit 58 at [Irving](/wiki/Irving,_New_York "Irving, New York"). After crossing [Cattaraugus Creek](/wiki/Cattaraugus_Creek "Cattaraugus Creek") into [Erie County](/wiki/Erie_County,_New_York "Erie County, New York"), the concurrency ends, with NY 5 staying close to the lake and US 20 closely paralleling the Thruway. [NY 438](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_438 "New York State Route 438") leaves this junction to the east, following the creek into [Cattaraugus Reservation](/wiki/Cattaraugus_Reservation,_Erie_County,_New_York "Cattaraugus Reservation, Erie County, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

US 20, which is now Southwestern Boulevard, remains straight when paralleling the Thruway, intersecting only one other state highway, [NY 249](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_249 "New York State Route 249"), just east of the village of [Farnham](/wiki/Farnham,_New_York "Farnham, New York"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] in an 18-mile (29 km) stretch that brings it to the outer fringes of the [Buffalo–Niagara Falls metropolitan area](/wiki/Buffalo%E2%80%93Niagara_Falls_metropolitan_area "Buffalo–Niagara Falls metropolitan area") at [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg,_New_York "Hamburg, New York"). Within two miles (3.2 km) of the junction with NY 75, which provides access to the Thruway, US 20 intersects [US 62](/wiki/U.S._Route_62_in_New_York "U.S. Route 62 in New York"), the first U.S. Highway that it encounters in New York. Continuing on, US 20 crosses the Thruway to reach [Big Tree](/wiki/Big_Tree,_New_York "Big Tree, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This hamlet was once home to a six-way intersection featuring [US 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)"),[[7]](#cite_note-buffalo_se_quad-7) but a late 2000s reconstruction project moved the west end of US 20A to a new junction to the northeast.[[8]](#cite_note-SWB-8)[[9]](#cite_note-SWB2-9) Like NY 39, US 20A makes a long loop through [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming_County,_New_York "Wyoming County, New York") and [Livingston](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York") counties to return to US 20 in the Finger Lakes.[[3]](#cite_note-2009map-3)

#### Buffalo area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Buffalo area")]

After the US 20A split, US 20 begins moving away from the lake and trends more to the east-northeast. The road becomes more developed, and, one mile (1.6 km) ahead, a major Buffalo landmark looms to the south—[Highmark Stadium](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson_Stadium "Ralph Wilson Stadium"), home of the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills"). On home game days in the fall, traffic clogs the highway as it crosses over the [US 219](/wiki/U.S._Route_219_in_New_York "U.S. Route 219 in New York") expressway and curves northeast to reach the eastern terminus of [NY 179](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_179 "New York State Route 179") at Mile Strip Road. NY 179 offers access to US 219 via an interchange 0.75 miles (1.21 km) to the west. A half-mile (0.80 km) beyond, the four-lane US 20 intersects the concurrent routes of [NY 240](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_240 "New York State Route 240") and [NY 277](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_277 "New York State Route 277"), the main route to the village of [Orchard Park](/wiki/Orchard_Park_(village),_New_York "Orchard Park (village), New York") off to the south.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At the [West Seneca](/wiki/West_Seneca,_New_York "West Seneca, New York") town line, US 20 turns due east when Reserve Road merges in from the west. After another mile (1.6 km), the road makes a 90-degree left turn at [NY 187](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_187 "New York State Route 187")'s northern terminus. It is now headed due north as Transit Road, a busy regional route that continues north as far as [Lockport](/wiki/Lockport_(city),_New_York "Lockport (city), New York"). After yet another mile (1.6 km), [NY 16](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_16 "New York State Route 16") and [NY 78](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_78 "New York State Route 78") come in from [East Aurora](/wiki/East_Aurora,_New_York "East Aurora, New York") to the southeast. NY 16 continues west to downtown Buffalo while NY 78 turns north to join US 20. Just north of the junction, Transit Road connects to the entrance ramps for an interchange with the [NY 400](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_400 "New York State Route 400") freeway, US 20's first interchange with a [limited-access highway](/wiki/Limited-access_highway "Limited-access highway") since meeting the Thruway in Irving.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Shops and stores line busy Transit Road on both sides as it divides the towns of West Seneca and [Elma](/wiki/Elma,_New_York "Elma, New York"). A mile (1.6 km) north of NY 400, US 20 and NY 78 reach another state highway, [NY 354](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_354 "New York State Route 354"), near [Ebenezer](/wiki/Ebenezer,_New_York "Ebenezer, New York"). Not far from that intersection, [Cheektowaga](/wiki/Cheektowaga_(town),_New_York "Cheektowaga (town), New York") and [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_New_York "Lancaster, New York") replace West Seneca and Elma as the towns on either side of the road. Increasing development and traffic heralds Transit Road's entry into the village of [Depew](/wiki/Depew,_New_York "Depew, New York"), and, at [NY 130](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_130 "New York State Route 130"), US 20 turns east, ending the overlap and taking the direction it will follow for most of the rest of its crossing of the state.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### East of Buffalo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: East of Buffalo")]

Taking the name Broadway, which NY 130 had retained from the city of Buffalo, US 20 goes through downtown Depew before it proceeds into the neighboring village of [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster_(village),_New_York "Lancaster (village), New York"). It returns to a rural two-lane road beyond Lancaster, passing through a traffic light only at the small village of [Alden](/wiki/Alden_(village),_New_York "Alden (village), New York") before crossing into [Genesee County](/wiki/Genesee_County,_New_York "Genesee County, New York"). In the town of [Darien](/wiki/Darien,_New_York "Darien, New York"), US 20 continues straight past [Darien Lakes State Park](/wiki/Darien_Lakes_State_Park "Darien Lakes State Park") to the junction with [NY 77](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_77 "New York State Route 77") at Darien Center, where much summertime traffic turns north toward nearby [Six Flags Darien Lake](/wiki/Six_Flags_Darien_Lake "Six Flags Darien Lake"). [NY 238](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_238 "New York State Route 238") forks off to the southeast towards [Attica](/wiki/Attica_(village),_New_York "Attica (village), New York") at a junction two miles (3.2 km) to the east of Darien Center.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![A road under a blue sky with white clouds goes past a farm on the left with rolls of hay in its fields and a red barn. In the distance the road climbs a small rise](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/US_20_west_of_Alexander%2C_NY.jpg/250px-US_20_west_of_Alexander%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_20_west_of_Alexander,_NY.jpg)

Dip in Genesee County

Past the junction with NY 238, which forks off to the southeast toward Attica, the highway begins going straight up and down into the creek valleys here at the northern fringe of the [Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Allegheny_Plateau "Allegheny Plateau"). Passing lanes are sometimes available on the downhill sections, particularly in the next community to the east, [Alexander](/wiki/Alexander_(village),_New_York "Alexander (village), New York"), where another route to Attica, [NY 98](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_98 "New York State Route 98") crosses via an overpass.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This allows traffic to continue at a high speed through the surrounding [dairy](/wiki/Dairy_farming "Dairy farming") and [sod](/w/index.php?title=Sod_farming&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sod farming (page does not exist)") farms up the other side and into [Bethany](/wiki/Bethany,_New_York "Bethany, New York"), the next town, whose four four-way intersections offer only blinking yellow lights and no stop or yield traffic control devices to slow down drivers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This unbroken stretch ends with a new light shortly into the next town, [Pavilion](/wiki/Pavilion,_New_York "Pavilion, New York"), where [NY 63](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_63 "New York State Route 63") crosses at an angle.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Here, traffic bound for the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier") and further turn right to eventually connect to [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") at [Mount Morris](/wiki/Mount_Morris_(village),_New_York "Mount Morris (village), New York").[[3]](#cite_note-2009map-3) The four-lane US 20 straightaway continues across the town, with [NY 19](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_19 "New York State Route 19") also on an overpass at Pavilion Center. US 20 goes on past the [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York") line 2.5 miles (4.0 km) to the east, where it finally drops the Broadway name. After another two miles (3.2 km), the route enters the hamlet of [Fraser](/wiki/Fraser,_Livingston_County,_New_York "Fraser, Livingston County, New York"), where the north–south [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") crosses US 20 on its way from Mount Morris to [Caledonia](/wiki/Caledonia_(village),_New_York "Caledonia (village), New York"). US 20 continues on to the community of [Canawaugus](/wiki/Canawaugus,_New_York "Canawaugus, New York") six miles (9.7 km) to the east, where NY 5 enters from the village of Caledonia to the northwest and rejoins US 20. The two routes continue east from here to the village of [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### NY 5 concurrency and Finger Lakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: NY 5 concurrency and Finger Lakes")]

The 67-mile (108 km) concurrency that begins here is the longest in the state,[[1]](#cite_note-2014tdr-1) and it carries the two routes across the northern [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes") region, a section of the plateau where [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") carved deep valleys only to fill them with their meltwater. The land around the road is gently hilly, often wide open and heavily farmed, on the outermost fringes of the [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") area. [NY 39](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_39 "New York State Route 39") is the first highway that intersects the concurrency, ending its loop just west of the [traffic circle](/wiki/Traffic_circle "Traffic circle") at Avon's center. About two miles (3.2 km) east of Avon, NY 5 and US 20 intersect [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15"), followed shortly by I-390, a north–south highway connecting US 20 and NY 5 to [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") in the north and [Corning](/wiki/Corning_(city),_New_York "Corning (city), New York") in the south. In the village of [Lima](/wiki/Lima_(village),_New_York "Lima (village), New York") five miles (8.0 km) ahead, NY 5 and US 20 meet [NY 15A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15A "New York State Route 15A"), the eastern alternate route of NY 15. Just east of the village, the road crosses [Honeoye Creek](/wiki/Honeoye_Creek "Honeoye Creek") and enters [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/US_20_and_NY_5_at_NY_444.jpg/250px-US_20_and_NY_5_at_NY_444.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_20_and_NY_5_at_NY_444.jpg)

US 20 eastbound at NY 444 near Bloomfield

Across the county line, US 20 and NY 5 pass through the town and hamlet of [West Bloomfield](/wiki/West_Bloomfield,_New_York "West Bloomfield, New York"), where [NY 65](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_65 "New York State Route 65") comes to an end from the left. Next, in neighboring [East Bloomfield](/wiki/East_Bloomfield,_New_York "East Bloomfield, New York"), [NY 444](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_444 "New York State Route 444") leaves to the north for [Victor](/wiki/Victor_(village),_New_York "Victor (village), New York") and [NY 64](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_64 "New York State Route 64") joins the concurrency. NY 64 leaves three miles (4.8 km) later at the eastern end of US 20A in South Bloomfield. The highway continues through the open, rolling lake country to a junction with West Avenue west of the city of [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua,_New_York "Canandaigua, New York"). At this point, NY 5 and US 20 turn southward to follow a [bypass](/wiki/Bypass_(road) "Bypass (road)") around the western and southern edge of the city. At Bristol Road, the first highway that the bypass crosses, [NY 21](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_21 "New York State Route 21") joins US 20 and NY 5 and follows both routes to Canandaigua, where a hill west of downtown offers a view to [Canandaigua Lake](/wiki/Canandaigua_Lake "Canandaigua Lake"), the first of the lakes it passes in this region.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In downtown Canandaigua, NY 21 leaves to join [NY 332](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_332 "New York State Route 332"), a prime route from Canandaigua and the lake region to the eastern suburbs of Rochester via [I-490](/wiki/Interstate_490_(New_York) "Interstate 490 (New York)"). Past NY 332, the highway runs past the lowlands abutting the lakes to a junction with [NY 364](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_364 "New York State Route 364"), which heads south down the east side of the lake. Now named Eastern Boulevard, the routes continue past [Finger Lakes Community College](/wiki/Finger_Lakes_Community_College "Finger Lakes Community College") to the next southbound highway, [NY 247](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_247 "New York State Route 247"). Roughly 10 miles (16 km) of open road climb the gentle rise dividing Canandaigua and [Seneca](/wiki/Seneca_Lake_(New_York) "Seneca Lake (New York)") lakes, the latter of which is the largest of the Finger Lakes. This ascent is subsequently met by a descent, passing over [Flint Creek](/wiki/Flint_Creek_(New_York) "Flint Creek (New York)") as the highway heads toward [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva,_New_York "Geneva, New York").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Just outside the city in the Lenox Park neighborhood of the town of [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva_(town),_New_York "Geneva (town), New York"), another pair of routes, [NY 14A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14A "New York State Route 14A") and [NY 245](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_245 "New York State Route 245"), come to their northern end. After entering the city and passing the campus of [Hobart and William Smith Colleges](/wiki/Hobart_and_William_Smith_Colleges "Hobart and William Smith Colleges"), NY 5 and US 20 cross under NY 14A's parent route, [NY 14](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14 "New York State Route 14"), just before the lake shore. US 20 and NY 5 continue along the water as a four-lane road with a median and cross into [Seneca County](/wiki/Seneca_County,_New_York "Seneca County, New York") at the lake's outlet. As at Canandaigua, a southbound route, [NY 96A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_96A "New York State Route 96A"), leaves just past the lake. NY 5 and US 20 continue on, following the northern edge of the [Cayuga–Seneca Canal](/wiki/Cayuga%E2%80%93Seneca_Canal "Cayuga–Seneca Canal") toward the village of [Waterloo](/wiki/Waterloo_(village),_New_York "Waterloo (village), New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/West_of_Auburn%2C_NY.jpg/250px-West_of_Auburn%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:West_of_Auburn,_NY.jpg)

US 20, NY 5, and NY 414 in [Seneca Falls](/wiki/Seneca_Falls_(village),_New_York "Seneca Falls (village), New York")

About 3 miles (5 km) east of NY 96A, US 20 and NY 5 reach that route's parent route, [NY 96](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_96 "New York State Route 96"), in the center of Waterloo. Just outside the village, [NY 414](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_414 "New York State Route 414"), a major north–south highway, creates another three-route concurrency when it joins from the north. It lasts for just 2 miles (3.2 km) to the center of [Seneca Falls](/wiki/Seneca_Falls_(village),_New_York "Seneca Falls (village), New York"), home to [Women's Rights National Historical Park](/wiki/Women%27s_Rights_National_Historical_Park "Women's Rights National Historical Park") and the [National Women's Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Hall_of_Fame "National Women's Hall of Fame"). Here, NY 414 departs to the south to follow the lake down to [Watkins Glen](/wiki/Watkins_Glen,_New_York "Watkins Glen, New York") while NY 5 and US 20 turn to the northeast at Van Cleef Lake.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This heading lasts for three miles (4.8 km) until [NY 318](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_318 "New York State Route 318") ends to the west right before a four-way intersection at [NY 89](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_89 "New York State Route 89"). From here, the road runs straight in a slightly more easterly direction south of [Montezuma National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Montezuma_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Montezuma National Wildlife Refuge"), where the Thruway is briefly visible to the north, before entering [Cayuga County](/wiki/Cayuga_County,_New_York "Cayuga County, New York") as it crosses over the Cayuga–Seneca Canal. In Cayuga County, the highway briefly returns to the east before a junction with [NY 90](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_90 "New York State Route 90"). The highway subsequently turns southeast and back east over the next eight miles (13 km) as it approaches [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn,_New_York "Auburn, New York"). [NY 326](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_326 "New York State Route 326") departs to the south at the western city limits. Roughly one mile (1.6 km) into the city, the roadway briefly splits as [NY 34](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_34 "New York State Route 34") and [NY 38](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_38 "New York State Route 38") briefly join. At [NY 38A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_38A "New York State Route 38A"), the overlap between NY 5 and US 20 comes to an end as the two routes go their separate ways.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Central New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Central New York")]

While NY 5 heads north from the split toward [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York") and the Thruway, US 20 keeps to its easterly heading, following East Genesee Street out of the city, proceeding past [Auburn Correctional Facility](/wiki/Auburn_Correctional_Facility "Auburn Correctional Facility") to the [Onondaga County](/wiki/Onondaga_County,_New_York "Onondaga County, New York") line. Here, [NY 41A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_41A "New York State Route 41A") comes in from the south at the western end of the village of [Skaneateles](/wiki/Skaneateles_(village),_New_York "Skaneateles (village), New York"), a community at the north end of [the eponymous lake](/wiki/Skaneateles_Lake "Skaneateles Lake"), the last Finger Lake along US 20. [NY 321](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_321 "New York State Route 321") leaves to the north in the center of town, and NY 41A's parent route, [NY 41](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_41 "New York State Route 41"), begins its long journey to the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier") on the other side of Skaneateles. US 20 continues east for another 1.25 miles (2.01 km) out of the village to the south end of [NY 175](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_175 "New York State Route 175"), after which it starts trending southward, returning to two lanes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![A two-lane road descending left to right across the bottom half of the image, with telephone poles along its left side, to a right turn. In the distance is a valley that extends to the rear of the field of view.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/US_20_view_at_Big_Bend%2C_NY.jpg/250px-US_20_view_at_Big_Bend%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_20_view_at_Big_Bend,_NY.jpg)

View at Big Bend, east of LaFayette

The landscape starts to grow hillier as it begins to cross the glacial [moraines](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine") of the less populated reaches of southern Onondaga County. About three miles (4.8 km) east of NY 175, US 20 is briefly joined by [NY 174](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_174 "New York State Route 174"), which goes to [Otisco Lake](/wiki/Otisco_Lake "Otisco Lake"), the easternmost Finger Lake. The southward trend continues for another six miles (9.7 km) to an intersection with [NY 80](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_80 "New York State Route 80"), a signed east–west route running north–south at this point. East of NY 80, it drops into the [Onondaga Creek](/wiki/Onondaga_Creek "Onondaga Creek") valley to connect to the northern terminus of [NY 11A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_11A "New York State Route 11A") four miles (6.4 km) to the east before climbing the next ridge to [LaFayette](/wiki/LaFayette,_New_York "LaFayette, New York"), where it intersects [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_New_York "Interstate 81 in New York") at exit 15 and meets its paralleling surface route, [US 11](/wiki/U.S._Route_11_in_New_York "U.S. Route 11 in New York") at junctions 0.5 miles (0.80 km) apart. At this point, US 20 is 15 miles (24 km) south of the Thruway.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From LaFayette, it drops into another deep valley, curving around an area known as Big Bend, prior to climbing back up five miles (8.0 km) towards the crest of the ridge at [Pompey](/wiki/Pompey,_New_York "Pompey, New York"). Here, US 20 and [NY 91](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_91 "New York State Route 91") intersect at an elevation of 1,700 feet (520 m) above sea level.[[11]](#cite_note-jamesville_topo-11) After another two miles (3.2 km), the road drops down into the [Limestone Creek](/wiki/Limestone_Creek_(Chittenango_Creek_tributary) "Limestone Creek (Chittenango Creek tributary)") valley and crosses into [Madison County](/wiki/Madison_County,_New_York "Madison County, New York") as it curves back up into higher ground. Another two miles (3.2 km) brings US 20 to the eastern terminus of [NY 92](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_92 "New York State Route 92"), the south end of [Cazenovia Lake](/wiki/Cazenovia_Lake "Cazenovia Lake") and the village of [Cazenovia](/wiki/Cazenovia_(village),_New_York "Cazenovia (village), New York") itself. Its main street is marked by a brief concurrency with [NY 13](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_13 "New York State Route 13"), a long, curving north–south route that comes from the cities of [Ithaca](/wiki/Ithaca,_New_York "Ithaca, New York") and [Cortland](/wiki/Cortland,_New_York "Cortland, New York") to the south.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

East of Cazenovia, US 20 resumes its southward drift as it climbs into the high ground again and expands to four lanes, passing the small village of [Morrisville](/wiki/Morrisville,_New_York "Morrisville, New York") in the process. It descends from there to its next state highway, [NY 46](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_46 "New York State Route 46"), where it reverts to two lanes for a brief concurrency, after which NY 46 leaves for isolated [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton_(village),_New_York "Hamilton (village), New York"). The southern trend reverses itself after this junction, with first [NY 26](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_26 "New York State Route 26") and later [NY 12B](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_12B "New York State Route 12B") joining US 20 to create another three-route overlap. This persists for three very straight miles (4.8 km) until both leave to the north in the town of [Madison](/wiki/Madison_(town),_New_York "Madison (town), New York"). After another two miles (3.2 km), the highway crosses into [Oneida County](/wiki/Oneida_County,_New_York "Oneida County, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/US_20_in_Central_NY.jpg/250px-US_20_in_Central_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_20_in_Central_NY.jpg)

Typically rugged stretch of US 20 in Central New York

The major intersection here is [NY 12](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_12 "New York State Route 12"), another long north–south route, three miles (4.8 km) into the county at [Sangerfield](/wiki/Sangerfield,_New_York "Sangerfield, New York"), one mile (1.6 km) south of [Waterville](/wiki/Waterville,_New_York "Waterville, New York"). From this intersection, US 20 takes the name Cherry Valley Turnpike from the historic road it follows towards Albany. US 20 climbs back into the high ground afterward and veers further southward to the next intersection with a major north–south highway, [NY 8](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_8 "New York State Route 8"), at [Bridgewater](/wiki/Bridgewater_(CDP),_New_York "Bridgewater (CDP), New York"). Like NY 12, it leads northward to the city of [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York"). After NY 8, the road crosses the headwaters of the [Unadilla River](/wiki/Unadilla_River "Unadilla River") into [Otsego County](/wiki/Otsego_County,_New_York "Otsego County, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Leatherstocking Region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Leatherstocking Region")]

US 20 enters Otsego County only for a mile (1.6 km), where it intersects [County Route 18](/wiki/County_Route_18_(Otsego_County,_New_York) "County Route 18 (Otsego County, New York)") (CR 18) which is the northern terminus of former [NY 413](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_413 "New York State Route 413"), then enters [Herkimer County](/wiki/Herkimer_County,_New_York "Herkimer County, New York") next to the point where Herkimer, Otsego, and Oneida county lines converge. The highway is briefly in Herkimer County for about 173 feet (53 m), before entering into Oneida County. US 20 continues for about 0.6 miles (0.97 km) before entering back into Herkimer County, which brings it into the [Leatherstocking Region](/w/index.php?title=Leatherstocking_Region&action=edit&redlink=1 "Leatherstocking Region (page does not exist)"), where [James Fenimore Cooper](/wiki/James_Fenimore_Cooper "James Fenimore Cooper") set his *[Leatherstocking Tales](/wiki/Leatherstocking_Tales "Leatherstocking Tales")*. This section of the highway keeps trending south as it skirts the northern edge of the [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains"), offering scenic valley vistas to the south. To the north the land levels to the [Mohawk Valley](/wiki/Mohawk_Valley "Mohawk Valley"). Through much of this stretch, the road expands to four lanes as the region's main east–west artery.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As it enters the county, it enters [West Winfield](/wiki/West_Winfield,_New_York "West Winfield, New York") and begins to overlap [NY 51](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_51 "New York State Route 51") which comes in from the south. This overlap lasts for three miles (4.8 km) to Birmingham Corners, where NY 51 turns north toward [Ilion](/wiki/Ilion,_New_York "Ilion, New York"), as US 20 continues east and soon reenters Otsego County. Trending southeast in the town of [Richfield](/wiki/Richfield,_New_York "Richfield, New York"), six miles (9.7 km) pass before [NY 28](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_28 "New York State Route 28") briefly overlaps US 20 just west of [Richfield Springs](/wiki/Richfield_Springs,_New_York "Richfield Springs, New York"), a popular local shopping destination, on its long curve across the state from the Catskill Mountains to the [Adirondack Mountains](/wiki/Adirondack_Mountains "Adirondack Mountains"). [NY 167](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_167 "New York State Route 167") begins a few blocks further east, and US 20 returns to Herkimer County one mile (1.6 km) beyond that, intersecting only county roads over the next several miles.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A mile (1.6 km) after returning to Otsego County, NY 80 again intersects on its way from [Cooperstown](/wiki/Cooperstown,_New_York "Cooperstown, New York") and [Otsego Lake](/wiki/Otsego_Lake_(New_York) "Otsego Lake (New York)") to the south to [Fort Plain](/wiki/Fort_Plain,_New_York "Fort Plain, New York") to the north. The next seven miles (11 km) of US 20 swing around the north end of the east ridge of Cherry Valley into the [town of that name](/wiki/Cherry_Valley,_New_York "Cherry Valley, New York"), where [NY 166](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_166 "New York State Route 166") leaves at a partial interchange to follow the valley south to [Milford](/wiki/Milford_(village),_New_York "Milford (village), New York"). After another swing around the valley's other ridge, US 20 crosses into [Schoharie County](/wiki/Schoharie_County,_New_York "Schoharie County, New York"). About two miles (3.2 km) from the county line, the highway intersects another long north–south route, [NY 10](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_10 "New York State Route 10"), south of [Sharon Springs](/wiki/Sharon_Springs,_New_York "Sharon Springs, New York"). US 20 proceeds for another five miles (8.0 km) to [Sharon](/wiki/Sharon,_New_York "Sharon, New York"), where [NY 145](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_145 "New York State Route 145") provides a route to [Cobleskill](/wiki/Cobleskill_(village),_New_York "Cobleskill (village), New York") to the south. Shortly after this junction, US 20 straightens out and follows an almost due-east heading for 11 miles (18 km) to its intersection with [NY 30A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_30A "New York State Route 30A") and the southern terminus of [NY 162](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_162 "New York State Route 162") at Sloansville. The route continues east to [Esperance](/wiki/Esperance_(village),_New_York "Esperance (village), New York"), where it crosses [Schoharie Creek](/wiki/Schoharie_Creek "Schoharie Creek") to enter [Schenectady County](/wiki/Schenectady_County,_New_York "Schenectady County, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Capital District

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Capital District")]

The crossing of the Schoharie Creek brings US 20 into the first of three counties that make up the [Capital District](/wiki/Capital_District_(New_York) "Capital District (New York)") area. Here, development along the road increases and the road itself is often four lanes. The region is divided by the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), where US 20 begins its second-longest concurrency with [US 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9_in_New_York "U.S. Route 9 in New York"), the state's longest north–south U.S. Highway.[[3]](#cite_note-2009map-3)

#### West of the Hudson River and Albany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: West of the Hudson River and Albany")]

Just past the county line, another Catskills-to-Canada highway, [NY 30](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_30 "New York State Route 30"), intersects with US 20. About three miles (4.8 km) beyond that is the northern terminus of short [NY 395](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_395 "New York State Route 395"), which runs south to the ever-closer [NY 7](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_7 "New York State Route 7"). US 20 veers closer to NY 7 before finally intersecting the highway three miles (4.8 km) from NY 395. Not far to the east is an interchange with [I-88](/wiki/Interstate_88_(New_York) "Interstate 88 (New York)"), NY 7's paralleling freeway between [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York") and [Schenectady](/wiki/Schenectady,_New_York "Schenectady, New York"). Access to I-88 is via a shared access road from both routes due to the oblique angle of the intersection.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] From here, the highway, now known as Western Turnpike, continues to trend to the south for another four miles (6.4 km) to the southern terminus of [NY 406](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_406 "New York State Route 406") at the hamlet of Gifford. Shortly afterward, US 20 enters [Albany County](/wiki/Albany_County,_New_York "Albany County, New York") as the [Helderberg Escarpment](/wiki/Helderberg_Escarpment "Helderberg Escarpment") looms to the south.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

It stays straight to its first major intersection, the northern terminus of [NY 397](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_397 "New York State Route 397") a mile (1.6 km) later, which is followed two miles (3.2 km) later by a junction with [NY 158](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_158 "New York State Route 158") just east of [Watervliet Reservoir](/w/index.php?title=Watervliet_Reservoir&action=edit&redlink=1 "Watervliet Reservoir (page does not exist)"). After another 1.5 miles (2.4 km), CSX Transportation's [main line](/wiki/Main_line_(railway) "Main line (railway)") crosses over the highway again. Development begins to pick up where [NY 146](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_146 "New York State Route 146") comes in from the north for a short overlap that ends at Hartmans Corners and Tawasentha Park. The road reaches the Albany suburbs two miles (3.2 km) to the east, where [NY 155](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_155 "New York State Route 155") intersects US 20 in [Westmere](/wiki/Westmere,_New_York "Westmere, New York"). Just beyond the community is [Crossgates Mall](/wiki/Crossgates_Mall "Crossgates Mall"), a major Albany-area destination. Immediately east of the mall is US 20's first crossing of the Thruway, now [I-87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)"), since the southern Buffalo suburbs, and the southern end of [Fuller Road Alternate](/wiki/NY_910F "NY 910F"), an unsigned spur of the [Adirondack Northway](/wiki/Adirondack_Northway "Adirondack Northway"), in an area known as [McKownville](/wiki/McKownville,_New_York "McKownville, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A half-mile (0.80 km) later, the busy four-lane route passes the south side of the [State University of New York at Albany](/wiki/University_at_Albany,_SUNY "University at Albany, SUNY")'s highly [modernistic](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture") campus. It continues into the city of Albany itself as Western Avenue. Commercial developments line both sides of US 20 to the interchange with [NY 85](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_85 "New York State Route 85"), a truncated freeway that connects to US 20 via the adjacent Dayton and Ormond streets. The neighborhoods around the road become primarily residential at this point. Roughly one mile (1.6 km) from NY 85, US 20 forks right onto [Madison Avenue](/wiki/Streets_of_Albany,_New_York#Madison_Avenue "Streets of Albany, New York") and passes the [College of Saint Rose](/wiki/College_of_Saint_Rose "College of Saint Rose").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/US20DMBridge.jpg/250px-US20DMBridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:US20DMBridge.jpg)

US 20 as it crosses the Dunn Memorial Bridge along with US 9

After [Washington Park](/wiki/Washington_Park_Historic_District_(Albany,_New_York) "Washington Park Historic District (Albany, New York)") on the north side, downtown Albany draws nearer, with [Erastus Corning Tower](/wiki/Erastus_Corning_Tower "Erastus Corning Tower"), the tallest building in the state outside of New York City, rising ahead. [US 9W](/wiki/U.S._Route_9W "U.S. Route 9W") crosses Madison from [Delaware Avenue](/wiki/Streets_of_Albany,_New_York#Delaware_Avenue "Streets of Albany, New York") to [Lark Street](/wiki/Lark_Street "Lark Street"), with its concurrent [NY 443](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_443 "New York State Route 443") terminating on the southern side of the intersection where Delaware ends at Madison. Shortly afterward, US 20 runs along the south side of [Empire State Plaza](/wiki/Empire_State_Plaza "Empire State Plaza"), the modern complex that houses the offices of [state legislators](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature") and many [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch") agencies. A few blocks further is [South Pearl Street](/wiki/Pearl_Street_(Albany) "Pearl Street (Albany)"), also [NY 32](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_32 "New York State Route 32"), a long route from the lower Hudson Valley to [Glens Falls](/wiki/Glens_Falls,_New_York "Glens Falls, New York"). US 20 follows two blocks of South Pearl Street and NY 32 to reach an interchange with the [South Mall Arterial](/wiki/South_Mall_Arterial "South Mall Arterial"). US 20 leaves NY 32 here, following an eastern extension of the arterial to the complex interchange connecting [I-787](/wiki/Interstate_787 "Interstate 787") with [US 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9_in_New_York "U.S. Route 9 in New York") and US 20. US 9 joins US 20 amid the junction, and both routes crosses the Hudson via the [Dunn Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Dunn_Memorial_Bridge "Dunn Memorial Bridge"), the southernmost free crossing of the Hudson.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### East of the Hudson River: US 9 and the Berkshires

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: East of the Hudson River: US 9 and the Berkshires")]

At the east end of the bridge, US 9 and US 20, now in the [city](/wiki/Rensselaer,_New_York "Rensselaer, New York") and [county](/wiki/Rensselaer_County,_New_York "Rensselaer County, New York") of Rensselaer, follow the offramps past the stubs built for the canceled South Mall Expressway and assume a southeastern course along a busy four-lane strip containing a center turn lane. Near the city limit, [NY 9J](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_9J "New York State Route 9J") leaves to the south to follow the river's east bank down to [Stockport](/wiki/Stockport,_New_York "Stockport, New York"). The road subsequently goes up a hill with a panoramic view of the Albany skyline behind it and proceeds into the town of [East Greenbush](/wiki/East_Greenbush,_New_York "East Greenbush, New York"), where [NY 151](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_151 "New York State Route 151") heads off to the northeast. After two miles (3.2 km) with heavy commercial development on either side of the road, [US 4](/wiki/U.S._Route_4_in_New_York "U.S. Route 4 in New York") begins and leaves to the north at a busy intersection.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [center of town](/wiki/East_Greenbush_(CDP),_New_York "East Greenbush (CDP), New York") follows, after which the concurrency enters [Schodack](/wiki/Schodack,_New_York "Schodack, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/US9US20Merge.jpg/250px-US9US20Merge.jpg)](/wiki/File:US9US20Merge.jpg)

US 20 merges here with US 9 in Schodack

In Schodack, development along the road begins to ease as the center turn lane gives way to a double yellow line. At Schodack Center, located four miles (6.4 km) from East Greenbush, I-90 and US 20 meet again for the first time in 300 miles (480 km). Just after the interchange, [NY 150](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_150 "New York State Route 150") comes to its southern end and US 9 and US 20 split at junctions less than 0.5 miles (0.80 km) apart. While US 9 heads south for [Poughkeepsie](/wiki/Poughkeepsie,_New_York "Poughkeepsie, New York") and the city of [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), US 20 reverts to two lanes and maintains its southeastern course for another two miles (3.2 km) before turns more to the east for another two miles (3.2 km). In [Nassau](/wiki/Nassau_(village),_New_York "Nassau (village), New York"), it connects to the north end of [NY 203](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_203 "New York State Route 203"). East of the village, the terrain becomes hillier, indicating that US 20 has reached the edge of the [Berkshires](/wiki/Berkshires "Berkshires").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

About two miles (3.2 km) outside of the village of Nassau, [NY 66](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_66 "New York State Route 66") joins the road. This overlap ends just before the [Columbia County](/wiki/Columbia_County,_New_York "Columbia County, New York") line, with NY 66 following a southerly course toward [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham_(village),_New_York "Chatham (village), New York"). US 20, meanwhile follows [Kinderhook](/wiki/Kinderhook_Creek "Kinderhook Creek") and [Wyomanock](/w/index.php?title=Wyomanock_Creek&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wyomanock Creek (page does not exist)") creeks, through narrow, wooded valleys to [New Lebanon](/wiki/New_Lebanon,_New_York "New Lebanon, New York"), passing [Mount Lebanon Shaker Society](/wiki/Mount_Lebanon_Shaker_Society "Mount Lebanon Shaker Society"), a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") as the second-oldest Shaker settlement in the country. In the center of this small town, US 20 meets [NY 22](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_22 "New York State Route 22"), the longest north–south route in the state, and the two form a one-mile (1.6 km) overlap, US 20's last concurrency in New York. After the split, US 20 makes a wide turn and heads almost south up a mountainside, climbing into Massachusetts near [Pittsfield State Forest](/wiki/Pittsfield_State_Forest "Pittsfield State Forest") a mile (1.6 km) later.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Origins")]

The road from [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") heading west was first constructed as a [toll road](/wiki/Toll_road "Toll road") or turnpike at the end of the 18th century. The **First Great Western Turnpike Corporation** was chartered in 1799 to build a road from Albany, the capital of New York, to the Revolutionary War frontier town of [Cherry Valley](/wiki/Cherry_Valley_(village),_New_York "Cherry Valley (village), New York").[[12]](#cite_note-cvt-12) In 1803, a second corporation, the **Third Great Western Turnpike**, was chartered to further extend the road to [Cazenovia](/wiki/Cazenovia_(village),_New_York "Cazenovia (village), New York").[[13]](#cite_note-us20preserve-13) The Third Great Western Turnpike was completed as planned by 1811 and was heavily used by people trying to establish new settlements in central New York. The two turnpikes, collectively known as the Cherry Valley Turnpike, became a stagecoach route in 1816. The Cherry Valley Turnpike name was also later applied to an untolled extension of the road west to [Skaneateles](/wiki/Skaneateles_(village),_New_York "Skaneateles (village), New York").[[14]](#cite_note-crookedlake-14)

The establishment of the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") and the [Utica and Schenectady Railroad](/wiki/Utica_and_Schenectady_Railroad "Utica and Schenectady Railroad") slowly ate away at the revenues of the Cherry Valley Turnpike. The turnpike stopped being a toll road in 1857.[[14]](#cite_note-crookedlake-14) A similar turnpike east of the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), connecting [Rensselaer](/wiki/Rensselaer,_New_York "Rensselaer, New York") to the [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") state line was also established in 1799 as the Rensselaer and Columbia Turnpike.[[15]](#cite_note-15) This turnpike remained in operation as late as 1896,[[16]](#cite_note-16) but it eventually folded around the turn of the 20th century. The state took over the maintenance of highways at the beginning of the 20th century and began paving many roads across the state. In 1908, the legislature created a statewide system of unsigned [legislative routes](/wiki/Legislative_route "Legislative route") that used these new state highways and other, yet-to-be improved roads.[[17]](#cite_note-1909book-17)

### Early designations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Early designations")]

Several parts of the former Cherry Valley and Rensselaer and Columbia turnpikes were included in the new route system. East of the Hudson River, the Rensselaer and Columbia Turnpike became part of Route 1 from [Schodack Center](/wiki/Schodack_Center,_New_York "Schodack Center, New York") to Rensselaer and part of Route 21 from [Nassau](/wiki/Nassau_(village),_New_York "Nassau (village), New York") east to the Massachusetts state line. West of Albany, the Cherry Valley Turnpike was designated as part of Route 9 from Cazenovia to [Bouckville](/wiki/Bouckville,_New_York "Bouckville, New York") and as part of Route 8 from there to a point south of [Oriskany Falls](/wiki/Oriskany_Falls,_New_York "Oriskany Falls, New York"). Another section, from [Bridgewater](/wiki/Bridgewater_(CDP),_New_York "Bridgewater (CDP), New York") to East Winfield, was included in Route 23.[[17]](#cite_note-1909book-17)[[18]](#cite_note-1920book-18) In 1912, the segment of the turnpike between [Sharon](/wiki/Sharon,_New_York "Sharon, New York") and [Sharon Springs](/wiki/Sharon_Springs,_New_York "Sharon Springs, New York") became part of the new Route 38-a.[[18]](#cite_note-1920book-18)

Farther west, Route 6 and Route 18 were assigned in 1908 to two major east–west roads connecting [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") to Albany and the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border"), respectively.[[17]](#cite_note-1909book-17)[[18]](#cite_note-1920book-18) West of modern-day [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York"), Route 6 followed the old [Genesee Road](/wiki/Genesee_Road "Genesee Road"), a highway that passed through [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn,_New_York "Auburn, New York"), [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva,_New_York "Geneva, New York"), [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York"), and [Batavia](/wiki/Batavia_(city),_New_York "Batavia (city), New York") on its way to Buffalo.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The easternmost leg of Route 6 in [Guilderland](/wiki/Guilderland,_New_York "Guilderland, New York") and Albany used a portion of the Cherry Valley Turnpike. Route 18, meanwhile, utilized a series of highways that ran either along or slightly inland from [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie").[[17]](#cite_note-1909book-17)[[18]](#cite_note-1920book-18) All of Route 18 and most of Route 6—including all of the route west of Auburn—later became part of the [Yellowstone Trail](/wiki/Yellowstone_Trail "Yellowstone Trail"),[[20]](#cite_note-1926map-20) an early cross-country [auto trail](/wiki/Auto_trail "Auto trail") established in 1912.[[21]](#cite_note-21) East of Albany, the trail utilized the old Rensselaer and Columbia Turnpike.[[20]](#cite_note-1926map-20)

On March 1, 1921, the legislature redefined the legislative route system, altering or removing several routes and adding others. Routes 6 and Route 18 were left virtually unchanged, as was the portion of Route 1 north of Schodack Center. Route 21, however, was altered to follow modern [NY 66](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_66 "New York State Route 66") between [Averill Park](/wiki/Averill_Park,_New_York "Averill Park, New York") and East Nassau. In the [Central New York Region](/wiki/Central_New_York_Region "Central New York Region"), Route 38-a was redesignated as Route 39 and extended west along the Cherry Valley Turnpike to meet Route 8 near Oriskany Falls. From Bridgewater to Oriskany Falls, Route 39 [overlapped](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with Route 23.[[22]](#cite_note-1921book-22) The legislative route system remained New York's primary route numbering system until the mid-1920s.[[23]](#cite_note-1924nyt-23)

### Assignment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Assignment")]

Highways were first publicly numbered in New York in 1924. One of the handful of routes assigned at this time was [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5"), a cross-state route that extended from the Pennsylvania line in the west to the Massachusetts line in the east. From Auburn west, it initially utilized all of legislative Route 18 and the portion of Route 6 east of Avon. In between Buffalo and Avon, NY 5 used a linear, previously unnumbered east–west road to the south that bypassed the communities along Route 6. Continuing east from Auburn, NY 5 followed the Cherry Valley Turnpike (partially Route 6, Route 8, Route 9, Route 23, and Route 39) to Albany and the Rensselaer and Columbia Turnpike (partially Route 1 and Route 21) to Massachusetts.[[20]](#cite_note-1926map-20)[[23]](#cite_note-1924nyt-23)

One or two years later, however, the portion of NY 5 from Buffalo to Albany was shifted north to its modern alignment—mostly old legislative Route 6—and the former, more southerly route was designated as **New York State Route 7** (**NY 7**). Around the same time, a parallel route to the south, from Buffalo through [East Aurora](/wiki/East_Aurora,_New_York "East Aurora, New York") and [Geneseo](/wiki/Geneseo_(village),_New_York "Geneseo (village), New York") to near Avon, was designated as [NY 35](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_35_(mid-1920s%E2%80%931927) "New York State Route 35 (mid-1920s–1927)").[[20]](#cite_note-1926map-20) NY 7 and NY 35 were both short-lived designations as US 20 was assigned on November 11, 1926, as part of the establishment of the [U.S. Numbered Highway System](/wiki/U.S._Numbered_Highway_System "U.S. Numbered Highway System").[[2]](#cite_note-1926map-ush-2) When the U.S. Highway was first posted in New York in 1927, it was assigned to then-NY 5 from the Pennsylvania line to near [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg_(village),_New_York "Hamburg (village), New York"), a previously unnumbered but state-maintained highway between Hamburg and East Aurora, NY 35 from East Aurora to Geneseo, NY 7 between Skaneateles and Albany, and NY 5 from Albany to the Massachusetts line. US 20 overlapped with NY 5 between Avon and Skaneateles. The old NY 7 from Buffalo to Avon was renumbered to [NY 35](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_35_(1927_%E2%80%93_early_1940s) "New York State Route 35 (1927 – early 1940s)").[[20]](#cite_note-1926map-20)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Realignments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Realignments")]

In the [1930 state highway renumbering](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)"), an alternate route of US 20 between [Irving](/wiki/Irving,_New_York "Irving, New York") and Hamburg became NY 20B. While US 20 ran along the shore of Lake Erie between the two locations, NY 20B followed a more inland alignment that took it east of [Angola](/wiki/Angola,_New_York "Angola, New York").[[25]](#cite_note-1929map-25)[[26]](#cite_note-1930map-26) US 20 was realigned c. 1932 to follow NY 20B between the two locations. The former lakeside routing of US 20 became part of an extended NY 5. Around the same time, the portion of Southwestern Boulevard between US 20 and [NY 78](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_78 "New York State Route 78") (now [NY 187](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_187 "New York State Route 187")) in the towns of [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg,_New_York "Hamburg, New York") and [Orchard Park](/wiki/Orchard_Park_(town),_New_York "Orchard Park (town), New York") was designated as part of [NY 18C](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_18C "New York State Route 18C").[[27]](#cite_note-1931map-g-27)[[28]](#cite_note-1932map-28) This segment of NY 18C was renumbered to NY 278 c. 1935.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-1935map-30) Farther east, US 20 was realigned c. 1931 to pass through [Lakeville](/wiki/Lakeville,_New_York "Lakeville, New York") on its way from Geneseo to East Avon.[[26]](#cite_note-1930map-26)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The most substantial realignment in US 20's New York history occurred c. 1938 when it was shifted north onto its current alignment between Hamburg and East Avon, replacing NY 278 and NY 35. Most of the previous route via East Aurora and Geneseo initially became [NY 20A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_20A_(1938%E2%80%931939) "New York State Route 20A (1938–1939)");[[32]](#cite_note-1937map-32)[[33]](#cite_note-1938map-33) however, that route was partially redesignated as [US 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)") by the following year.[[34]](#cite_note-1939map-34) In the mid-1950s, a new four-lane roadway, Eastern Boulevard, was built north of Lake Shore Drive between South Main Street in [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua,_New_York "Canandaigua, New York") and the modern-day intersection of Lake Shore and Eastern Boulevard in [Hopewell](/wiki/Hopewell,_New_York "Hopewell, New York"). US 20 and NY 5 were moved from Lake Shore Drive to the new highway upon completion.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) The remainder of the bypass around the southwestern extents of the city was built in the late 1970s and early 1980s.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In Canandaigua, the former routing of US 20 on South Main Street remains state maintained as [NY 942T](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_942T "New York State Route 942T");[[6]](#cite_note-2009log-6) until 1996, the portion of West Avenue between the west end of the bypass and the Canandaigua city line (also old US 20) was also maintained by [NYSDOT](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") as [NY 942W](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_942W "New York State Route 942W"). Even though maintenance of the road was transferred to the town of [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(town),_New_York "Canandaigua (town), New York") on September 1, 1996,[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) the designation remained in NYSDOT documents until 2007.[[41]](#cite_note-2007tdr-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) In Hamburg, the section of old US 20 between Athol Springs and [Big Tree](/wiki/Big_Tree,_New_York "Big Tree, New York") that did not become part of NY 5 c. 1932 is now [CR 576](/wiki/County_Route_576_(Erie_County,_New_York) "County Route 576 (Erie County, New York)") from NY 5 to Bayview Road and NY 951E, an unsigned [reference route](/wiki/List_of_reference_routes_in_New_York "List of reference routes in New York"), from US 62 to US 20A.[[6]](#cite_note-2009log-6)[[43]](#cite_note-erie_roads-43)

In Geneva, US 20 was initially routed on East North Street and Border City Road, overlapping with [NY 14](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14 "New York State Route 14") through the city and rejoining its modern routing in East Geneva.[[26]](#cite_note-1930map-26) The overlap was eliminated c. 1931 when US 20 was moved onto a new roadway located along the edge of [Seneca Lake](/wiki/Seneca_Lake_(New_York) "Seneca Lake (New York)").[[26]](#cite_note-1930map-26)[[27]](#cite_note-1931map-g-27) US 20 was realigned again in the 1960s to use a new [divided highway](/wiki/Divided_highway "Divided highway") built midway between the lake shore road and Border City Road.[[44]](#cite_note-1962map-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Border City Road is now maintained by Seneca County as CR 110.[[46]](#cite_note-geneva_north_quad-46)

### Construction and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Construction and legacy")]

[![Scenic Byway sign](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Scenic_Byway_plate_brown.svg/120px-Scenic_Byway_plate_brown.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Scenic_Byway_plate_brown.svg)

[![US 20 Scenic Byway sign](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/eb/US_20_Scenic_Byway_New_York.svg/120px-US_20_Scenic_Byway_New_York.svg.png)](/wiki/File:US_20_Scenic_Byway_New_York.svg)

Signage for the Route 20 Scenic Byway

While most of US 20 was constructed in the early 20th century as part of the legislative route system, many sections of the highway were left unimproved or unbuilt until well into the 1930s. Two substantial sections of the route—from [Alexander](/wiki/Alexander_(village),_New_York "Alexander (village), New York") ([NY 98](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_98 "New York State Route 98")) to Avon and from Auburn to Cazenovia—were unimproved or unbuilt as late as 1926.[[20]](#cite_note-1926map-20) The portion from Auburn to Skaneateles was brought up to state highway standards c. 1930[[25]](#cite_note-1929map-25)[[26]](#cite_note-1930map-26) while the section connecting [LaFayette](/wiki/LaFayette,_New_York "LaFayette, New York") ([US 11](/wiki/U.S._Route_11_in_New_York "U.S. Route 11 in New York")) to [Pompey](/wiki/Pompey,_New_York "Pompey, New York") ([NY 91](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_91 "New York State Route 91")) was built by the following year.[[26]](#cite_note-1930map-26)[[27]](#cite_note-1931map-g-27) The last section of the Skaneateles–Cazenovia segment to open was the piece between Pompey and Cazenovia, which opened to traffic in mid-September 1934.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Meanwhile, the highway was brought up to standards from Alexander to [Bethany](/wiki/Bethany,_New_York "Bethany, New York") c. 1934,[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-1934map-49) from Bethany to [Pavilion](/wiki/Pavilion,_New_York "Pavilion, New York") ([NY 19](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_19 "New York State Route 19")) c. 1935,[[30]](#cite_note-1935map-30)[[49]](#cite_note-1934map-49) between Pavilion and [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") c. 1936,[[30]](#cite_note-1935map-30)[[50]](#cite_note-1936map-50) and from NY 36 to Avon c. 1937.[[50]](#cite_note-1936map-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The now-complete US 20 was the major thoroughfare across New York for several decades. During the early days, there were no motels around and only major cities—such as Auburn and Albany—had hotels. Around the late 1940s, automobiles became much more reliable and US 20 grew into a strong touring road. The road was one of the most traveled up until the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") was created in the 1950s. Much of traffic and progress along US 20 were halted as a result.[[52]](#cite_note-nyt1-52) The 372-mile (599 km) US 20 is lined with historical places, including [Sharon Springs](/wiki/Sharon_Springs,_New_York "Sharon Springs, New York"), [Cherry Valley](/wiki/Cherry_Valley_(village),_New_York "Cherry Valley (village), New York"), [Bouckville](/wiki/Bouckville,_New_York "Bouckville, New York"), Cazenovia, Skaneateles, Auburn, Geneva, and East Aurora. The length of US 20 from LaFayette to [Duanesburg](/wiki/Duanesburg,_New_York "Duanesburg, New York") was designated as a 108-mile (174 km) New York State Scenic Byway on August 10, 2005.[[53]](#cite_note-byway-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

## Suffixed routes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20_in_New_York&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Suffixed routes")]

US 20 has had seven suffixed routes in New York; one [U.S. Highway](/wiki/U.S._Highway "U.S. Highway") (US 20A) and six New York state routes. All six state routes have been removed or redesignated; US 20A, however, still exists.

* [US 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)") (83.59 mi or 134.53 km) is an alternate route of US 20 between [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg,_New_York "Hamburg, New York"), [Erie County](/wiki/Erie_County,_New_York "Erie County, New York"), and [East Bloomfield](/wiki/East_Bloomfield,_New_York "East Bloomfield, New York"), [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York").[[1]](#cite_note-2014tdr-1) It was assigned c. 1939.[[33]](#cite_note-1938map-33)[[34]](#cite_note-1939map-34)
* The NY 20A designation has been used for two distinct highways:
  + The [first NY 20A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_20A_(1930%E2%80%931932) "New York State Route 20A (1930–1932)") was an alternate route of US 20 between the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") at [Ripley](/wiki/Ripley,_New_York "Ripley, New York") and [Silver Creek](/wiki/Silver_Creek,_New_York "Silver Creek, New York") in [Chautauqua County](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York"). It was assigned as part of the [1930 state highway renumbering](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)")[[26]](#cite_note-1930map-26) and supplanted by [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") c. 1932.[[27]](#cite_note-1931map-g-27)[[28]](#cite_note-1932map-28)
  + The [second NY 20A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_20A_(1938%E2%80%931939) "New York State Route 20A (1938–1939)") was a short-lived alternate route of US 20 between Hamburg and [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York"). It was assigned c. 1938[[32]](#cite_note-1937map-32)[[33]](#cite_note-1938map-33) and largely replaced by US 20A by the following year.[[34]](#cite_note-1939map-34)
* NY 20B was an alternate route of US 20 between [Irving](/wiki/Irving,_New_York "Irving, New York") and [Orchard Park](/wiki/Orchard_Park_(town),_New_York "Orchard Park (town), New York") in Erie County. It was assigned as part of the 1930 renumbering[[26]](#cite_note-1930map-26) and supplanted by a realigned US 20 c. 1932.[[27]](#cite_note-1931map-g-27)[[28]](#cite_note-1932map-28)
* [NY 20C](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_20C "New York State Route 20C") was a loop route connecting US 20 to the then-village of [Holcomb](/wiki/Holcomb,_New_York "Holcomb, New York") (now part of [Bloomfield](/wiki/Bloomfield,_New_York "Bloomfield, New York")) in [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York"). The route was assigned c. 1931[[26]](#cite_note-1930map-26)[[27]](#cite_note-1931map-g-27) and partially replaced by [NY 444](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_444 "New York State Route 444") in 1997.[[6]](#cite_note-2009log-6)
* [NY 20D](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_20D "New York State Route 20D") was a connector between then-US 20 in [Geneseo](/wiki/Geneseo,_New_York "Geneseo, New York") and [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") in [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York"). It was assigned c. 1931[[26]](#cite_note-1930map-26)[[27]](#cite_note-1931map-g-27) and supplanted by NY 20A c. 1938. The route is now the northeasternmost portion of [NY 39](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_39 "New York State Route 39").[[33]](#cite_note-1938map-33)[[34]](#cite_note-1939map-34)
* [NY 20N](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_20N "New York State Route 20N") was an alternate route of US 20 between [Marcellus](/wiki/Marcellus,_New_York "Marcellus, New York"), [Onondaga County](/wiki/Onondaga_County,_New_York "Onondaga County, New York"), and [Cazenovia](/wiki/Cazenovia_(village),_New_York "Cazenovia (village), New York"), [Madison County](/wiki/Madison_County,_New_York "Madison County, New York"). The route overlapped other, preexisting routes for its entire length. It was assigned c. 1938[[32]](#cite_note-1937map-32)[[55]](#cite_note-55) and removed c. 1962, leaving just the routes it overlapped.[[44]](#cite_note-1962map-44)[[56]](#cite_note-1961map-56)
* [NY 20SY](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_20SY "New York State Route 20SY") was an alternate route of US 20 between [Skaneateles](/wiki/Skaneateles_(village),_New_York "Skaneateles (village), New York") and Cazenovia in Onondaga and Madison counties. Like NY 20N, NY 20SY was concurrent with other, preexisting routes for most of its length. It was assigned in the early 1950s[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) and removed c. 1962, leaving just the routes it overlapped.[[44]](#cite_note-1962map-44)[[56]](#cite_note-1961map-56)

**U.S. Route 20 Truck** (**US 20 Truck**) is a short [truck route](/wiki/Truck_route "Truck route") of US 20 in [Silver Creek](/wiki/Silver_Creek,_New_York "Silver Creek, New York"). The road begins at an intersection with US 20 in Silver Creek, goes along Central Avenue for 0.05 miles (0.080 km) as NY 952H and merges in with NY 5. The two become concurrent for another mile (1.6 km) on Howard Street where US 20 Truck ends at an intersection with US 20.

In the village of Silver Creek, commercial truck traffic is not allowed to use US 20 east unless said traffic is making deliveries to local businesses in either the village or the South Dayton, Perrysburg, and Cherry Creek areas southeast of Silver Creek. All other commercial truck traffic per New York state vehicle and traffic laws must use US 20 Truck through the village.
